title: Snowflake Metrics Analyst — Table, View, and Semantic Model Import
story: As a data analyst, I want to select a Snowflake table, view, or semantic model and run it through Metrics Analyst, so that I can automatically discover and catalog numeric metrics from my data warehouse.
description:
Extend Metrics Analyst to support Snowflake as a data source. Users can select a Snowflake table or view, batch-process all numeric fields (int, float), and receive AI-analyzed metric suggestions.

### Workflow

1. **Select Source** — User chooses a Snowflake table, view, or semantic model
2. **Batch Analysis** — Instead of processing fields one-by-one, send the full field list to an OpenAI agent that returns results in the standard metrics JSON format
3. **Present Results** — Display suggestions using the refined Metrics Analyst interface (Lovable mockup) for quick add/merge actions

### Architecture
- OpenAI calls are routed through a DataTools Pro backend service (not called directly from JavaScript)
- The wizard should support selecting: Table, View, or Semantic Model as source types
- Multiple semantic views can roll up into the metrics glossary
- Scheduled sync capability to continuously monitor for changes in Snowflake semantic models

### JSON Payload Format
The payload sent to OpenAI includes metric objects with name, description, definition, business_owner, target_interval, date_field_name, aliases, taxonomy (Line of Business), and logic (system + logic_definition referencing Snowflake table.column paths).

### Strategic Vision
This positions DataTools Pro as a primary semantics and metrics management tool for Snowflake, enabling import from multiple layers with merge and reconciliation capabilities.
acceptance_criteria: User can select a Snowflake table, view, or semantic model as the analysis source
All numeric fields (int, float) from the selected source are batch-processed through the AI analyst
OpenAI calls are routed through the DataTools Pro backend service, not called from JavaScript directly
Results are displayed in the refined Metrics Analyst interface with add/merge actions
JSON payload includes metric name, description, aliases, taxonomy, and Snowflake logic definitions
Semantic views can be imported and their metrics merged into the glossary
ux_changes: New source selector for Snowflake tables/views/semantic models in Metrics Analyst. Refined results interface (Lovable mockup) for reviewing and adding/merging discovered metrics.
middleware_changes: Backend service to proxy OpenAI requests with Snowflake field metadata. Batch processing of numeric fields. Metric merge and reconciliation logic using similar_text matching with configurable thresholds.
data_model_changes: Metrics glossary extended to store Snowflake logic definitions (system + table.column paths). Semantic view association with metrics, facts, and dimensions stored in dedicated models.
api_changes: New backend endpoint to accept Snowflake metadata payload and return AI-analyzed metric suggestions. REST API integration with Snowflake SQL statement API for semantic view operations.
implementation_notes: JSON payload structure shared with metric objects containing name, description, definition, business_owner, target_interval, date_field_name, aliases, taxonomy, and logic arrays. Semantic view management includes import, refresh (with last-refresh timestamp), and potential export back to Snowflake via CREATE OR REPLACE SEMANTIC VIEW SQL. Match scoring uses PHP similar_text() function comparing metric names and aliases. 100% match suppresses 'Create as alias' option. Ryan suggested exploring a Snowflake native app extension to bridge DataTools Pro and Snowflake for executing semantic view updates.
comment_summary: Waqar shared the JSON payload structure and implemented metric import from Snowflake semantic views with metrics/facts/dimensions storage. Extensive discussion about semantic view CRUD operations — Waqar implemented SQL-based semantic view creation and view/refresh functionality. Ryan expanded scope to include Table/View/Semantic Model selection and raised concerns about executing SQL in customer Snowflake instances, suggesting a native app bridge. Match scoring and alias creation logic were refined with percentage thresholds.
